items.title: Blurred Lines: AdTech Abuse Delivers Browser Hijackers Through the Microsoft Store
items.description: A newly uncovered campaign abuses the Trillion (formerly Trellian) AdTech network, mimicking the flow of a Traffic Direction System (TDS) to trick visitors of typo-squatted domains into downloading Microsoft Store apps that contain browser hijacking malware. While the abuse of AdTech networks to deliver malware isn’t new, this campaign highlights incredibly similar tactics to VexTrio and previous
items.ai_summary: The threat involves abusing the Trillion AdTech network to deliver browser hijackers through Microsoft Store apps, mimicking a Traffic Direction System to trick visitors into downloading malware. This campaign highlights similar tactics to VexTrio, indicating a potential resurgence of AdTech abuse. Defenders should prioritize monitoring Microsoft Store app downloads and AdTech network activity for suspicious behavior.

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Signed malware impersonating workplace apps deploys RMM backdoors
items.description: Multiple phishing campaigns were identified using workplace meeting lures, PDF attachments, and abuse of legitimate binaries to deliver signed malware. The attacks used digitally signed executables masquerading as legitimate software to install remote monitoring and management (RMM) tools like ScreenConnect, Tactical RMM, and Mesh Agent. These tools enabled attackers to establish persistence and m
items.ai_summary: The threat involves signed malware masquerading as legitimate workplace software, delivered through phishing campaigns, to deploy RMM backdoors. This allows attackers to establish persistence and gain remote access to compromised systems. Defenders should prioritize verifying the authenticity of software and attachments to prevent initial access.

items.title: Securing the Supply Chain: How SentinelOne's AI EDR Stops the ...
items.description: On March 31, 2026, a North Korean state actor hijacked the npm credentials of the primary Axios maintainer and published two backdoored releases that deployed a cross-platform remote access trojan (RAT) to Windows, macOS, and Linux systems. Axios is the most widely used HTTP client in the JavaScript ecosystem, with approximately 100 million weekly downloads and a presence in roughly 80% of cloud a
items.ai_summary: The threat is a supply chain compromise where a North Korean state actor hijacked the npm credentials of the primary Axios maintainer, deploying a cross-platform remote access trojan to Windows, macOS, and Linux systems. This has a significant real-world impact due to Axios' widespread use in the JavaScript ecosystem. The most important defender action is to immediately update Axios to a version not affected by the backdoor.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

items.title: A Technique-Based Approach to Hunting Web-Delivered Malware
items.description: This report presents a technique-based approach to HTTP body hunting using Censys that addresses this tension directly, and demonstrates its effectiveness by walking through a live discovery: a ClickFix campaign delivering XWorm V5.6 through a 5-stage attack chain.
items.ai_summary: The threat involves a 5-stage attack chain delivering XWorm V5.6 malware through a ClickFix campaign, utilizing HTTP body hunting tactics. The real-world impact includes potential data breaches and system compromises. The most important defender action is to implement robust vulnerability scanning and patch management to prevent initial access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1595.002| B[Discovery]:::tactic
    B -->|T1566| C[Initial Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: DPRK-Related Campaigns with LNK and GitHub C2
items.description: FortiGuard Labs recently detected a series of LNK files targeting users in South Korea. These attacks use a multi-stage scripting process and leverage GitHub as Command and Control (C2) infrastructure to evade detection. Although these LNK files can be traced back to 2024, earlier versions had less obfuscation and contained significant metadata, allowing us to track similar attacks spreading the X
items.ai_summary: The threat involves a multi-stage scripting process using LNK files to target users in South Korea, leveraging GitHub as Command and Control infrastructure to evade detection. The attacks utilize various TTPs, including web services and obfuscated files, to impair defenses and establish multi-stage channels. To defend against this threat, it is essential to implement robust email and web filtering to block malicious LNK files and monitor for suspicious GitHub activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1027| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1102| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1583.006| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1562| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Iranian APT Infrastructure in Focus: Mapping State-Aligned Clusters During Geopolitical Escalation
items.description: The analysis examines Iranian state-aligned threat actors and their infrastructure patterns during heightened geopolitical tensions. It focuses on mapping network infrastructure, ASN patterns, TLS fingerprints, and hosting clusters associated with various Iranian APT groups. The report highlights the importance of proactive infrastructure monitoring to detect and disrupt potential cyber operations
items.ai_summary: Iranian state-aligned threat actors are utilizing various tactics to gather victim host information and steal or forge authentication certificates, posing a significant threat to organizations. The threat actors' infrastructure patterns, including network infrastructure and hosting clusters, can be monitored to detect and disrupt potential cyber operations. To defend against these threats, organizations should prioritize proactive infrastructure monitoring and implement robust authentication mechanisms.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1592| B[Discovery]:::tactic
    B -->|T1649| C[Credential Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: MuddyWater Exposed: Inside an Iranian APT operation
items.description: Researchers identified and analyzed exposed infrastructure of MuddyWater, an Iranian cyber espionage group linked to the Ministry of Intelligence and Security. The investigation revealed their reconnaissance methods, exploitation of vulnerabilities, custom command and control frameworks, and exfiltration techniques. Targets included organizations in Israel, Jordan, Egypt, UAE, Portugal, and the US
items.ai_summary: MuddyWater, an Iranian APT, uses reconnaissance, exploits vulnerabilities, and custom C2 frameworks to exfiltrate data from targets in multiple countries. Their tactics include Application Layer Protocol and Exfiltration Over C2 Channel. Defenders should monitor for suspicious network activity and implement vulnerability patching.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Reconnaissance]:::tactic
    B -->|T1041| C[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: South American telecommunication providers targeted with three new malware implants
items.description: UAT-9244, a China-nexus advanced persistent threat actor, has been targeting critical telecommunications infrastructure in South America since 2024. The group employs three new malware implants: TernDoor, a Windows-based backdoor variant of CrowDoor; PeerTime, an ELF-based backdoor using BitTorrent protocol; and BruteEntry, a brute force scanner for SSH, Postgres, and Tomcat servers. UAT-9244 uses
items.ai_summary: UAT-9244, a China-nexus APT, targets South American telecoms with three new malware implants: TernDoor, PeerTime, and BruteEntry. These implants enable backdoor access, brute force attacks, and remote access. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for T1190, T1110, and T1219 TTPs.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: An Investigation Into Years of Undetected Operations Targeting High-Value Sectors
items.description: Since 2020, a Chinese threat actor dubbed CL-UNK-1068 has been targeting high-value organizations across South, Southeast and East Asia, focusing on critical sectors like aviation, energy, government, and telecommunications. The group employs a diverse toolkit including custom malware, modified open-source utilities, and living-off-the-land binaries to maintain stealthy persistence. Their techniqu
items.ai_summary: CL-UNK-1068, a Chinese threat actor, has been undetected since 2020, targeting high-value sectors in Asia with custom malware, modified open-source utilities, and living-off-the-land binaries. Their techniques include exploiting public-facing applications and developing capabilities. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for these tactics to prevent stealthy persistence.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

items.title: Middle East Conflict Fuels Opportunistic Cyber Attacks
items.description: The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has triggered a surge in cybercriminal activity. Over 8,000 newly registered domains with conflict-related keywords have been identified, many of which may be weaponized in future campaigns. Multiple cases of malicious activity have been observed, including targeted attacks using conflict-themed lures, deployment of the LOTUSLITE backdoor, fake news blogs le
items.ai_summary: Cybercriminals are exploiting the Middle East conflict to launch opportunistic attacks, using conflict-themed lures and newly registered domains to deploy malware. The LOTUSLITE backdoor has been observed in targeted attacks. Defenders should monitor for conflict-related domains and block user execution of suspicious files.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-22744: In RedisFilterExpressionConverter of spring-ai-redis-store, when a user-controll...
items.description: In RedisFilterExpressionConverter of spring-ai-redis-store, when a user-controlled string is passed as a filter value for a TAG field, stringValue() inserts the value directly into the @field:{VALUE} RediSearch TAG block without escaping characters.This issue affects Spring AI: from 1.0.0 before 1.0.5, from 1.1.0 before 1.1.4.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-22744 is a remote code execution vulnerability in Spring AI's RedisFilterExpressionConverter, allowing an attacker to inject malicious code. This affects versions 1.0.0-1.0.5 and 1.1.0-1.1.4. To defend, update to a patched version of Spring AI.

items.title: CVE-2026-22743: Spring AI's spring-ai-neo4j-store contains a Cypher injection vulnerability in N...
items.description: Spring AI's spring-ai-neo4j-store contains a Cypher injection vulnerability in Neo4jVectorFilterExpressionConverter. When a user-controlled string is passed as a filter expression key in Neo4jVectorFilterExpressionConverter of spring-ai-neo4j-store, doKey() embeds the key into a backtick-delimited Cypher property accessor (node.`metadata.`) after stripping only double quotes, without escaping embe
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-22743 is a Cypher injection vulnerability in Spring AI's spring-ai-neo4j-store, allowing an attacker to inject malicious Cypher code. This can lead to unauthorized data access or modification. Defenders should update the affected component to the latest version.

items.title: CVE-2026-22742: Spring AI's spring-ai-bedrock-converse contains a Server-Side Request Forgery (S...
items.description:
Spring AI's spring-ai-bedrock-converse contains a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in BedrockProxyChatModel when processing multimodal messages that include user-supplied media URLs. Insufficient validation of those URLs allows an attacker to induce the server to issue HTTP requests to unintended internal or external destinations.
This issue affects Spring AI: from 1.0.0 before 1.0
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-22742 is a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in Spring AI's spring-ai-bedrock-converse, allowing attackers to induce the server to issue HTTP requests to unintended destinations. This issue affects Spring AI versions 1.0.0 and earlier. Defenders should update to the latest version to mitigate this vulnerability.

items.title: CVE-2026-22738: In Spring AI, a SpEL injection vulnerability exists in SimpleVectorStore when a ...
items.description:
In Spring AI, a SpEL injection vulnerability exists in SimpleVectorStore when a user-supplied value is used as a filter expression key. A malicious actor could exploit this to execute arbitrary code. Only applications that use SimpleVectorStore and pass user-supplied input as a filter expression key are affected.
This issue affects Spring AI: from 1.0.0 before 1.0.5, from 1.1.0 before 1.1.4.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-22738 is a critical SpEL injection vulnerability in Spring AI's SimpleVectorStore, allowing malicious actors to execute arbitrary code. This affects Spring AI versions 1.0.0 before 1.0.5 and 1.1.0 before 1.1.4. To defend, update to a patched version of Spring AI.

items.description:
Use after free vulnerability in Softing smartLink HW-DP or smartLink HW-PN webserver allows HTTP DoS.
This issue affects:
smartLink HW-DP: through 1.31
smartLink HW-PN: before 1.02.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2024-14028 is a use-after-free vulnerability in Softing smartLink HW-DP and HW-PN web servers, allowing HTTP DoS attacks. This issue affects smartLink HW-DP through 1.31 and smartLink HW-PN before 1.02. Defenders should patch affected systems to prevent exploitation.

items.title: CVE-2026-3098: The Smart Slider 3 plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Arbitrary File Read in ...
items.description: The Smart Slider 3 plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Arbitrary File Read in all versions up to, and including, 3.5.1.33 via the 'actionExportAll' function. This makes it possible for authenticated attackers, with Subscriber-level access and above, to read the contents of arbitrary files on the server, which can contain sensitive information.

items.description: The next major breach hitting your clients probably won't come from inside their walls. It'll come through a vendor they trust, a SaaS tool their finance team signed up for, or a subcontractor nobody in IT knows about. items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
items.description: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) code to the legitimate site and returning its responses.

items.title: Kimwolf Botnet Swamps Anonymity Network I2P
items.description: For the past week, the massive "Internet of Things" (IoT) botnet known as Kimwolf has been disrupting the The Invisible Internet Project (I2P), a decentralized, encrypted communications network designed to anonymize and secure online communications. I2P users started reporting disruptions in the network around the same time the Kimwolf botmasters began relying on it to evade takedown attempts against the botnet's control servers.
